critic_raw:
## ML Critique (Run 2): Sequence Models vs. Tabular Baselines for Readmission Prediction

**Issue 1 — Early post-discharge utilization feature leaks future information into discharge-time prediction**

The model is designed to support clinical intervention decisions at the time of discharge. The 'early post-discharge utilization signal' includes events that happen 0–7 days after discharge: outpatient follow-up, ED presentations, nurse triage contacts, medication reconciliation. At the moment of discharge, these events have not yet occurred. Including them as features means the training and evaluation datasets use information that would not be available when the model is actually run. This is classic temporal leakage — the feature is correlated with the 30-day outcome (patients who go to the ED within 7 days are at elevated readmission risk) because it is causally downstream of or contemporaneous with the factors causing readmission. A model trained with this feature will appear more accurate than it can be in deployment.

**Issue 2 — ClinicalBERT/SciBERT note embeddings may encode discharge diagnosis codes embedded in notes**

Clinical notes often contain diagnostic impressions, assessment sections, and plan of care that reference the primary diagnosis. If the sequence model encodes note embeddings that contain diagnostic text, and the ICD codes for that diagnosis are correlated with readmission risk, the model may be indirectly using diagnosis information in a richer form than the baseline. This is not leakage per se, but it means the comparison is not purely 'sequences vs. aggregates' — it is also 'richer text encoding vs. code-level aggregates.'
defender_raw:
## Design Defense (Run 2)

**Issue 1:** I concede. The 7-day post-discharge utilization signal is post-discharge information. At discharge time, these events have not occurred. Including this feature in a discharge-time prediction model constitutes temporal leakage. Both models are contaminated.

**Issue 2:** Note embeddings encoding richer clinical context is the intended design — the hypothesis is that rich text encoding outperforms code-level aggregation. If the transformer captures diagnostic implications in text that the baseline does not, that is the mechanism being tested. Defense wins — this is by design.
adjudication_raw:
## Adjudication — Run 2

- Issue 1: Critique wins (conceded). Post-discharge signal as feature at discharge-time prediction is target leakage.
- Issue 2: Defense wins. Text richness advantage is the mechanism under test.

**Verdict: critique_wins**
